title: Stable sort vs unstable sort problem
description: Understand and apply stable vs unstable sorting in a problem

examples.explanation:
Initial: [Bob(25), Alice(30), Charlie(25)]
Step 1 (i=0): Find min in [0..2] -> min_idx=0 (Bob, 25)
Swap arr[0] with arr[0] (no change)
Array: [Bob(25), Alice(30), Charlie(25)]
Step 2 (i=1): Find min in [1..2] -> Compare Alice(30) and Charlie(25) -> min_idx=2 (Charlie, 25)
Swap arr[1] (Alice) with arr[2] (Charlie)
Array: [Bob(25), Charlie(25), Alice(30)]
Step 3 (i=2): Loop ends (n-1=2 iterations)
Final: [Bob(25), Charlie(25), Alice(30)]
Note: Bob and Charlie both age 25, original order Bob then Charlie is NOT preserved (Charlie moved before Alice then swapped with Alice).

Initial: [3(5), 1(2), 2(5), 4(1)]
Step 1 (i=0): Find min in [0..3] -> Compare 3(5), 1(2), 2(5), 4(1) -> min_idx=3 (4, 1)
Swap arr[0] (3) with arr[3] (4)
Array: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Step 2 (i=1): Find min in [1..3] -> Compare 1(2), 2(5), 3(5) -> min_idx=1 (1, 2)
Swap arr[1] with arr[1] (no change)
Array: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Step 3 (i=2): Find min in [2..3] -> Compare 2(5) and 3(5) -> min_idx=2 (2, 5)
Swap arr[2] with arr[2] (no change)
Array: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Final: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Note: Elements with val=5 (id 3 and 2) - original order 3 then 2 is NOT preserved (3 moved to end).

approaches.name: Approach 1: Naive Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: This approach involves manually comparing and swapping elements to sort while ignoring stability, such as using a simple selection sort that does not preserve the relative order of equal elements. It is useful for understanding the basic concept of unstable sorting but is inefficient for practical applications.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement Unstable Selection Sort to Sort Array of Objects
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of unstable selection sort that sorts an array of objects by a key attribute, ignoring stability.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete problem solution using the naive brute force approach. The intuition is that we can sort by repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted portion and swapping it to the front, without caring about preserving the relative order of equal elements. This demonstrates the concept of unstable sorting in practice. The algorithm works by maintaining two parts of the array: sorted (left) and unsorted (right). In each iteration, we find the minimum element in the unsorted portion and swap it with the first element of the unsorted portion. This process continues until the entire array is sorted. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each of n elements, we scan up to n elements to find the minimum. Space complexity is O(1) as we sort in-place. This connects to the parent problem by showing how an unstable sort behaves when equal elements exist - their original relative order is not preserved. Common mistakes include: 1) Not updating the correct index after swapping, 2) Forgetting to handle the last element (it's already sorted), 3) Using stable comparison when equal elements should be considered equal. To avoid these, carefully track indices and remember that for unstable sort, we only care about the sorting key, not the original position. Real code pattern: def unstable_selection_sort(arr, key): n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): min_idx = i; for j in range(i+1, n): if arr[j][key] < arr[min_idx][key]: min_idx = j; arr[i], arr[min_idx] = arr[min_idx], arr[i]; return arr

examples.trace:
Initial: [Bob(25), Alice(30), Charlie(25)]
Step 1 (i=0): Find min in [0..2] -> min_idx=0 (Bob, 25)
Swap arr[0] with arr[0] (no change)
Array: [Bob(25), Alice(30), Charlie(25)]
Step 2 (i=1): Find min in [1..2] -> Compare Alice(30) and Charlie(25) -> min_idx=2 (Charlie, 25)
Swap arr[1] (Alice) with arr[2] (Charlie)
Array: [Bob(25), Charlie(25), Alice(30)]
Step 3 (i=2): Loop ends (n-1=2 iterations)
Final: [Bob(25), Charlie(25), Alice(30)]
Note: Bob and Charlie both age 25, original order Bob then Charlie is NOT preserved (Charlie moved before Alice then swapped with Alice).

Initial: [3(5), 1(2), 2(5), 4(1)]
Step 1 (i=0): Find min in [0..3] -> Compare 3(5), 1(2), 2(5), 4(1) -> min_idx=3 (4, 1)
Swap arr[0] (3) with arr[3] (4)
Array: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Step 2 (i=1): Find min in [1..3] -> Compare 1(2), 2(5), 3(5) -> min_idx=1 (1, 2)
Swap arr[1] with arr[1] (no change)
Array: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Step 3 (i=2): Find min in [2..3] -> Compare 2(5) and 3(5) -> min_idx=2 (2, 5)
Swap arr[2] with arr[2] (no change)
Array: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Final: [4(1), 1(2), 2(5), 3(5)]
Note: Elements with val=5 (id 3 and 2) - original order 3 then 2 is NOT preserved (3 moved to end).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Minimum Index in Unsorted Portion
ladders.desc: Implement a function that finds the index of the minimum element in a subarray.
ladders.explanation: This is the most important helper function for selection sort. The intuition is that to sort by repeatedly selecting minimums, we need an efficient way to find where the smallest element is located in the unsorted portion of the array. This sub-routine works by scanning through the specified range of indices, keeping track of the smallest element seen so far. We start by assuming the first element in the range is the minimum, then compare each subsequent element to update the minimum index when we find a smaller element. The time complexity is O(k) where k is the size of the subarray being scanned. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L0 by being the core operation repeated in each iteration of selection sort - without this, we couldn't implement the sort. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to initialize min_idx to the start index, 2) Using <= instead of < which affects stability (but for unstable sort we use <), 3) Not handling empty ranges. To avoid these, always validate input range and remember we want strictly smaller elements to update the minimum. Real code pattern: def find_min_index(arr, start, end, key): min_idx = start; for i in range(start+1, end+1): if arr[i][key] < arr[min_idx][key]: min_idx = i; return min_idx

examples.input: arr = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9], start = 0, end = 4, key = None (direct values)
examples.trace:
Initial: arr = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9], start=0, end=4
Set min_idx = 0 (value 5)
i=1: Compare arr[1]=2 < arr[0]=5 → Update min_idx=1
i=2: Compare arr[2]=8 < arr[1]=2 → No update
i=3: Compare arr[3]=1 < arr[1]=2 → Update min_idx=3
i=4: Compare arr[4]=9 < arr[3]=1 → No update
Return min_idx=3

Initial: arr indices 1-3: [{'v':5}, {'v':10}, {'v':3}], key='v'
Set min_idx = 1 (value 5)
i=2: Compare arr[2]['v']=10 < arr[1]['v']=5 → No update
i=3: Compare arr[3]['v']=3 < arr[1]['v']=5 → Update min_idx=3
Return min_idx=3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Minimum Selection and Swap
ladders.desc: Implement the main loop that repeatedly finds minimums and swaps them to the front.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of selection sort. The intuition is that by repeatedly selecting the smallest remaining element and moving it to its correct position, we can gradually build a sorted array from left to right. The algorithm works by maintaining a boundary between the sorted left portion and unsorted right portion. In each iteration, we find the minimum element in the unsorted portion and swap it with the first element of the unsorted portion, effectively extending the sorted portion by one element. This process continues until the entire array is sorted. The time complexity is O(n²) because we perform n iterations, each taking O(n) to find the minimum. Space complexity is O(1) as we sort in-place. This connects to L1 by using the find_min_index function in a loop. Common mistakes include: 1) Swapping before finding the minimum, 2) Incorrect loop boundaries (should be n-1 iterations), 3) Not updating indices correctly after swap. To avoid these, visualize the sorted/unsorted boundary and remember each iteration places one element in its final position. Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n-1): min_idx = find_min_index(arr, i, n-1, key); if i != min_idx: swap(arr[i], arr[min_idx])

examples.trace:
Initial: [4, 2, 3, 1]
Iteration 1 (i=0): Find min in [0..3] → min_idx=3 (value 1)
Swap arr[0] and arr[3] → [1, 2, 3, 4]
Iteration 2 (i=1): Find min in [1..3] → min_idx=1 (value 2)
No swap needed → [1, 2, 3, 4]
Iteration 3 (i=2): Find min in [2..3] → min_idx=2 (value 3)
No swap needed → [1, 2, 3, 4]
Final: [1, 2, 3, 4]

Initial: [3, 1, 2]
Iteration 1 (i=0): Find min in [0..2] → min_idx=1 (value 1)
Swap arr[0] and arr[1] → [1, 3, 2]
Iteration 2 (i=1): Find min in [1..2] → min_idx=2 (value 2)
Swap arr[1] and arr[2] → [1, 2, 3]
Final: [1, 2, 3]

ladders.title: Building Block — Compare and Swap Elements in Array
ladders.desc: Implement element comparison and swapping operations for array sorting.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves the fundamental operations needed for any comparison-based sort. The intuition is that sorting requires two basic operations: comparing elements to determine their order, and swapping elements to rearrange them. Comparison tells us whether elements are in the correct order, while swapping physically moves them. For unstable sorting, we only compare based on the key value, ignoring original positions. The algorithm works by: 1) Comparing two elements using their key values, 2) If they're out of order (based on sort criteria), swapping their positions in the array. Time complexity for a single compare is O(1), and for a swap is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) as we use temporary variables. This connects to L2 by providing the basic operations used in each iteration of selection sort. Common mistakes include: 1) Comparing objects incorrectly (need to access key attribute), 2) Swapping without temporary variable (can lose data), 3) Forgetting that swap changes array indices. To avoid these, always use explicit comparison on the key and implement swap carefully. Real code pattern: def compare(a, b, key): return a[key] < b[key]; def swap(arr, i, j): temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp

examples.trace:
a['age'] = 25, b['age'] = 30
25 < 30 → True
So a should come before b in ascending sort.

Initial: arr = [1, 2, 3]
temp = arr[0] = 1
arr[0] = arr[2] = 3
arr[2] = temp = 1
Result: [3, 2, 1]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access and Modify Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Work with array indexing to read and write elements at specific positions.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is essential for any array manipulation algorithm. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we access them using indices (positions). Reading an element tells us its value, while writing changes the array contents. For sorting, we need to read elements to compare them and write elements to swap them. The operation works by: 1) Using an index (integer) to specify position, 2) For reading: retrieving the value at that index, 3) For writing: assigning a new value to that index. Time complexity is O(1) for both operations. Space complexity is O(1) as we don't create new data structures. This connects to L3 by enabling the compare and swap operations - without array indexing, we couldn't implement sorting. Common mistakes include: 1) Using out-of-bounds indices (causing errors), 2) Confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, 3) Not understanding that array modification affects subsequent operations. To avoid these, always validate indices are within [0, len(arr)-1] and remember arrays are mutable. Real code pattern: element = arr[index] (read); arr[index] = new_value (write)

examples.trace:
Array indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30
Read arr[1] = 20

Initial: arr = [5, 6, 7]
Write arr[0] = 8
Array becomes: [8, 6, 7]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is an Array and How to Loop Through It
ladders.desc: Understand arrays as ordered collections and iterate through elements using loops.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for any sorting algorithm. The intuition is that an array is a fundamental data structure that stores multiple elements in a specific order, allowing us to work with collections of data. To process all elements in an array (like when sorting), we need to iterate through them systematically. Arrays work by storing elements at consecutive indices starting from 0. Looping allows us to visit each element one by one, either to read, compare, or modify them. The basic operation is using a for-loop with an index variable that goes from 0 to length-1, accessing each element via arr[index]. Time complexity for iterating through an array is O(n) where n is the number of elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself. This connects to L4 by providing the structure we operate on - without arrays and loops, we couldn't implement any sorting algorithm. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors (using <= instead of <), 2) Modifying array while iterating (can cause unexpected behavior), 3) Not understanding that arrays maintain order. To avoid these, remember array indices start at 0 and loops should typically use range(len(arr)). Real code pattern: arr = [element1, element2, ...]; for i in range(len(arr)): current = arr[i]

examples.input: Array: [3, 1, 4], iterate and print each element
examples.trace:
Array has length 3, indices 0,1,2
i=0: arr[0]=3 → print 3
i=1: arr[1]=1 → print 1
i=2: arr[2]=4 → print 4

Length=3
i=0: Access arr[0]='a'
i=1: Access arr[1]='b'
i=2: Access arr[2]='c'

approaches.name: Approach 2: Stable Sorting Algorithm O(n log n)
approaches.summary: This approach uses a stable sorting algorithm like merge sort or insertion sort to sort elements while preserving the relative order of equal elements. It is applied in scenarios where maintaining the original sequence of identical items is critical, such as in multi-key sorting problems.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for merge sort, O(n²) for insertion sort
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for merge sort, O(1) for insertion sort

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stable Sort Implementation for Multi-Key Sorting
ladders.desc: Implement a stable sorting algorithm to sort an array of objects by multiple keys while preserving relative order of equal elements.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires implementing a stable sorting algorithm to sort an array of objects by multiple keys. The key insight is that stable sorting preserves the relative order of elements that compare equal according to the primary sorting key, which is crucial when secondary keys need to maintain their original sequence. We'll use merge sort as our stable algorithm due to its O(n log n) time complexity and O(n) space complexity. The intuition is that when two elements have equal primary keys, their original order from the input array should be maintained in the sorted output. This property allows us to sort by multiple keys sequentially - first by the most significant key, then by secondary keys, without losing the ordering from previous sorts. The algorithm works by recursively dividing the array into halves until we reach single elements, then merging them back together while maintaining stability by preferring elements from the left half when keys are equal. Step-by-step: 1) If array has 0 or 1 elements, return it (base case). 2) Find the middle index. 3) Recursively sort left and right halves. 4) Merge the two sorted halves by comparing elements and always taking from the left half when keys are equal. Time complexity is O(n log n) for the divide-and-conquer approach, and space complexity is O(n) for the temporary arrays during merging. This connects to the parent problem by providing a complete solution to stable sorting for multi-key scenarios. Common mistakes include: 1) Using an unstable sort like quicksort, 2) Not handling equal elements correctly during merge, 3) Forgetting to preserve original indices. Real code pattern: def stable_sort(arr, key_func): if len(arr) <= 1: return arr; mid = len(arr)//2; left = stable_sort(arr[:mid], key_func); right = stable_sort(arr[mid:], key_func); return merge(left, right, key_func).

examples.trace: Input: [(5,'a'), (2,'b'), (5,'c'), (1,'d'), (2,'e')]. Step 1: Recursively divide: Left half [(5,'a'), (2,'b')], Right half [(5,'c'), (1,'d'), (2,'e')]. Step 2: Sort left half: Divide [(5,'a')] and [(2,'b')], merge comparing keys 5 and 2 -> [(2,'b'), (5,'a')]. Step 3: Sort right half: Divide [(5,'c')] and [(1,'d'), (2,'e')], sort right part to [(1,'d'), (2,'e')], merge with [(5,'c')] comparing 5,1,2 -> [(1,'d'), (2,'e'), (5,'c')]. Step 4: Merge halves: Compare (2,'b') and (1,'d') -> take (1,'d'). Compare (2,'b') and (2,'e') -> equal keys, take from left (2,'b'). Compare (5,'a') and (2,'e') -> take (2,'e'). Compare (5,'a') and (5,'c') -> equal keys, take from left (5,'a'). Add remaining (5,'c'). Final: [(1,'d'), (2,'b'), (2,'e'), (5,'a'), (5,'c')].

Input: [('b',3), ('a',1), ('b',2), ('c',4), ('a',5)]. Step 1: Divide: Left [('b',3), ('a',1)], Right [('b',2), ('c',4), ('a',5)]. Step 2: Sort left: Divide [('b',3)] and [('a',1)], merge comparing 'b' and 'a' -> [('a',1), ('b',3)]. Step 3: Sort right: Divide [('b',2)] and [('c',4), ('a',5)], sort right to [('a',5), ('c',4)], merge with [('b',2)] comparing 'b','a','c' -> [('a',5), ('b',2), ('c',4)]. Step 4: Merge halves: Compare ('a',1) and ('a',5) -> equal keys, take from left ('a',1). Compare ('b',3) and ('a',5) -> take ('a',5). Compare ('b',3) and ('b',2) -> equal keys, take from left ('b',3). Compare ('c',4) and ('b',2) -> take ('b',2). Add remaining ('c',4). Final: [('a',1), ('a',5), ('b',3), ('b',2), ('c',4)].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Merge Function for Stable Sorting
ladders.desc: Implement the merge function that combines two sorted arrays while preserving stability.
ladders.explanation: The merge function is the core operation in merge sort that combines two already-sorted arrays into one sorted array while maintaining stability. The intuition is that when merging, if two elements have equal keys, we must take the element from the left array first to preserve the original relative order. This ensures that elements that were earlier in the original input remain earlier in the output when their keys are equal. The algorithm works by using two pointers (i and j) to traverse the left and right arrays, comparing elements at each step. When left[i] <= right[j], we take left[i] (using <= instead of < is crucial for stability). When left[i] > right[j], we take right[j]. We continue until one array is exhausted, then append the remaining elements. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the total number of elements being merged, and space complexity is O(n) for the result array. This connects to level 0 as the essential component that enables the full merge sort algorithm to be stable. Common mistakes include: 1) Using < instead of <= when comparing, which breaks stability, 2) Not handling remaining elements, 3) Creating unnecessary copies. Real code pattern: def merge(left, right, key_func): result = []; i = j = 0; while i < len(left) and j < len(right): if key_func(left[i]) <= key_func(right[j]): result.append(left[i]); i += 1; else: result.append(right[j]); j += 1; result.extend(left[i:]); result.extend(right[j:]); return result.

examples.input: Merge left=[(2,'a'), (5,'b')] and right=[(2,'c'), (3,'d')] by first element
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 2: Compare left[0]=(2,'a') key=2 and right[0]=(2,'c') key=2: equal, use <= so take from left: add (2,'a'), i=1. Step 3: Compare left[1]=(5,'b') key=5 and right[0]=(2,'c') key=2: 5 > 2, take from right: add (2,'c'), j=1. Step 4: Compare left[1]=(5,'b') key=5 and right[1]=(3,'d') key=3: 5 > 3, take from right: add (3,'d'), j=2. Step 5: Right exhausted, add remaining left[1:] = [(5,'b')]. Final result: [(2,'a'), (2,'c'), (3,'d'), (5,'b')].

examples.input: Merge left=[(1,'x')] and right=[(1,'y'), (2,'z')] by first element
examples.trace: Step 1: i=0, j=0, result=[]. Step 2: Compare left[0]=(1,'x') key=1 and right[0]=(1,'y') key=1: equal, take from left: add (1,'x'), i=1. Step 3: Left exhausted, add remaining right[0:] = [(1,'y'), (2,'z')]. Final result: [(1,'x'), (1,'y'), (2,'z')].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Stability Property in Sorting
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the concept that equal elements maintain their original relative order after sorting.
ladders.explanation: The core logic of stable sorting is the property that when two elements have equal keys according to the sorting criteria, their relative order from the original input is preserved in the sorted output. The intuition is that stability matters when we care about more than just the primary sorting key - it allows us to perform multi-key sorting by sorting sequentially from least to most significant key. For example, if we sort students first by grade, then by name, a stable sort ensures that within each grade, students remain alphabetically ordered after the name sort. This works because when the grade sort is stable, students with the same grade keep their original order, which after the name sort becomes alphabetical order. The algorithm insight is that to achieve stability, when comparing elements during sorting operations, if keys are equal, we must prefer the element that appeared earlier in the original sequence. In merge sort, this is implemented by using <= instead of < during the merge step. Time complexity consideration: stability doesn't change the asymptotic complexity of sorting algorithms, but it may affect constant factors. Space complexity is unchanged. This connects to level 1 as the fundamental principle that guides how the merge function should behave. Common mistakes include: 1) Confusing stability with overall correctness, 2) Thinking any O(n log n) sort is automatically stable, 3) Not realizing when stability is needed. Real code pattern: The key is in comparison logic: if a.key == b.key: preserve original order (often by comparing indices or using stable algorithm).

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify elements with equal keys: (5,'apple') and (5,'cherry') both have key 5. Step 2: In original order: (5,'apple') comes before (5,'cherry'). Step 3: After stable sorting by number: All elements sorted by key 3 then 5. For key 5 elements, maintain original relative order: (5,'apple') before (5,'cherry'). Result: [(3,'banana'), (5,'apple'), (5,'cherry')]. If unstable, could be [(3,'banana'), (5,'cherry'), (5,'apple')].

Step 1: Equal keys: ('cat',10) and ('cat',30) both have key 'cat'. Step 2: Original order: ('cat',10) before ('cat',30). Step 3: Stable sort by animal: Sort all by animal 'cat' then 'dog'. For 'cat' elements, preserve original order: ('cat',10) before ('cat',30). Result: [('cat',10), ('cat',30), ('dog',20)].

ladders.title: Building Block — Divide and Conquer Recursion
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive division of an array into halves for merge sort.
ladders.explanation: Divide and conquer is the algorithmic paradigm where a problem is broken down into smaller subproblems, solved recursively, and then combined. For merge sort, the building block is recursively dividing the array into two halves until reaching base cases of 0 or 1 elements. The intuition is that sorting a single element is trivial (it's already sorted), and by breaking the problem down, we can eventually merge sorted subarrays back together efficiently. The algorithm works by: 1) Check base case: if array length ≤ 1, return array (already sorted). 2) Find the middle index: mid = len(arr) // 2. 3) Recursively sort the left half: left_sorted = sort(arr[:mid]). 4) Recursively sort the right half: right_sorted = sort(arr[mid:]). 5) Return the merge of left_sorted and right_sorted (which will be implemented in level 1). Time complexity analysis: Each division creates two subproblems of half size, leading to O(log n) levels of recursion, with O(n) work per level for merging, giving O(n log n) total. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursive call stack and temporary arrays. This connects to level 2 as the structural framework that enables the stability property to be applied through careful merging. Common mistakes include: 1) Not having a proper base case causing infinite recursion, 2) Incorrect midpoint calculation, 3) Creating too many array copies inefficiently. Real code pattern: def merge_sort(arr): if len(arr) <= 1: return arr; mid = len(arr)//2; left = merge_sort(arr[:mid]); right = merge_sort(arr[mid:]); return merge(left, right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Full array [5,2,8,1,9], length 5 > 1, mid=2. Step 2: Left half [5,2], length 2 > 1, mid=1: Left of left [5] (base case), Right of left [2] (base case). Step 3: Right half [8,1,9], length 3 > 1, mid=1: Left of right [8] (base case), Right of right [1,9] length 2 > 1, mid=1: Left of right-right [1] (base case), Right of right-right [9] (base case). Final base cases: [5], [2], [8], [1], [9].

Step 1: [3,1,4], length 3 > 1, mid=1. Step 2: Left half [3], length 1 (base case). Step 3: Right half [1,4], length 2 > 1, mid=1: Left [1] (base case), Right [4] (base case). Final base cases: [3], [1], [4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Slicing and Indexing
ladders.desc: Work with array subsets using slicing and access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: Array slicing and indexing are fundamental operations for manipulating collections of data. The intuition is that we need to access specific elements and create subarrays to implement divide-and-conquer algorithms. Indexing allows direct access to an element at a specific position (0-based), while slicing creates a new array containing a range of elements. The algorithm for slicing: arr[start:end] creates a new array with elements from index start (inclusive) to end (exclusive). If start is omitted, it defaults to 0; if end is omitted, it defaults to len(arr). Negative indices count from the end. For merge sort, we use slicing to divide the array: left = arr[:mid] takes elements from start to mid-1, right = arr[mid:] takes from mid to end. Time complexity of slicing is O(k) where k is the size of the slice, as it creates a copy. Space complexity is O(k) for the new array. This connects to level 3 as the mechanism for creating subarrays to recursively sort. Common mistakes include: 1) Off-by-one errors with slice boundaries, 2) Confusing inclusive/exclusive ranges, 3) Assuming slices are views rather than copies. Real code pattern: mid = len(arr)//2; left = arr[:mid]; right = arr[mid:]; element = arr[0].

examples.trace: Step 1: Original array indices: 0:10, 1:20, 2:30, 3:40, 4:50. Step 2: Slice arr[1:4] means start at index 1 (inclusive), end at index 4 (exclusive). Step 3: Extract elements at indices 1,2,3: 20,30,40. Result: [20,30,40].

examples.input: Array [5,2,8,1], find middle and create halves
examples.trace: Step 1: Array length = 4. Step 2: Calculate mid = 4//2 = 2. Step 3: Left half arr[:2] means indices 0 to 1 (exclusive of 2): indices 0:5, 1:2 -> [5,2]. Step 4: Right half arr[2:] means indices 2 to end: indices 2:8, 3:1 -> [8,1]. Result: Left [5,2], Right [8,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is Sorting?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic idea of rearranging elements into a specific order.
ladders.explanation: Sorting is the process of arranging elements in a particular order, typically ascending or descending, based on some key or comparison criteria. The intuition is that ordered data is easier to search, analyze, and process. For example, finding a number in a sorted list can be done quickly with binary search, while in an unsorted list requires checking every element. The basic algorithm concept involves comparing elements pairwise and swapping them if they're out of order, repeating until all elements are in the correct sequence. For beginners, think of sorting books on a shelf by height: you look at two books, if the taller one is to the left of the shorter one, you swap them, and continue until all books are from shortest to tallest. Time complexity varies by algorithm from O(n²) for simple methods to O(n log n) for efficient ones. Space complexity can be O(1) for in-place sorts or O(n) for those needing extra storage. This connects to level 4 as the fundamental goal that all subsequent operations work toward. Common mistakes include: 1) Not defining what 'order' means clearly, 2) Confusing sorting with searching, 3) Overlooking that elements may have multiple attributes to sort by. Real code pattern: The simplest bubble sort: for i in range(n): for j in range(n-1): if arr[j] > arr[j+1]: swap(arr[j], arr[j+1]).

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 5 and 2: 5 > 2, so swap -> [2,5,8,1]. Step 2: Compare 5 and 8: 5 < 8, no swap. Step 3: Compare 8 and 1: 8 > 1, swap -> [2,5,1,8]. Step 4: Pass 2: Compare 2 and 5: 2 < 5, no swap. Step 5: Compare 5 and 1: 5 > 1, swap -> [2,1,5,8]. Step 6: Compare 5 and 8: 5 < 8, no swap. Step 7: Pass 3: Compare 2 and 1: 2 > 1, swap -> [1,2,5,8]. Step 8: Compare 2 and 5: 2 < 5, no swap. Step 9: Compare 5 and 8: 5 < 8, no swap. Final sorted: [1,2,5,8].

Step 1: Compare 'cat' and 'apple': 'c' > 'a', so swap -> ['apple','cat','dog']. Step 2: Compare 'cat' and 'dog': 'c' < 'd', no swap. Step 3: Pass 2: Compare 'apple' and 'cat': 'a' < 'c', no swap. Step 4: Compare 'cat' and 'dog': 'c' < 'd', no swap. Final sorted: ['apple','cat','dog'].

approaches.name: Approach 3: Custom Stable Sort with Key Augmentation O(n log n)
approaches.summary: This approach modifies an unstable sorting algorithm, such as quicksort or heapsort, by augmenting each element with its original index as a secondary key to enforce stability during comparison. It allows leveraging efficient unstable sorts while achieving stability, useful when a specific unstable algorithm is preferred for performance reasons.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stable Sort Implementation with Key Augmentation
ladders.desc: Complete solution using custom stable sort with index augmentation
ladders.explanation: This full problem implements a stable sorting solution by augmenting an unstable sorting algorithm with original indices to preserve order of equal elements. The intuition is that while algorithms like quicksort or heapsort are unstable (they may reorder equal elements arbitrarily), we can enforce stability by adding a secondary key - the original index - that breaks ties during comparisons. This approach gives us the performance benefits of efficient unstable sorts while achieving stability. The algorithm works by: 1) Creating augmented elements that pair each value with its original index, 2) Sorting these augmented elements using an unstable sort with a custom comparator that first compares values, then indices for equal values, 3) Extracting the sorted values from the augmented elements. The time complexity is O(n log n) for the sorting step plus O(n) for augmentation and extraction, resulting in O(n log n) overall. Space complexity is O(n) for storing the augmented array. This connects to the broader problem of understanding stable vs unstable sorts by providing a practical implementation that transforms an unstable sort into a stable one. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the index comparison properly in the comparator, not preserving the original indices correctly, or trying to modify the sorting algorithm's internal logic instead of using augmentation. The key insight is that stability is about preserving relative order of equal elements, which can be enforced by making each element unique through augmentation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Original array: [3, 1, 2, 1, 4]
Step 2: Create augmented array with indices: [(3,0), (1,1), (2,2), (1,3), (4,4)]
Step 3: Sort using custom comparator (value first, then index):
   Compare (1,1) vs (1,3): values equal (1=1), compare indices 1<3, so (1,1) comes first
   Compare (1,1) vs (2,2): 1<2, so (1,1) comes first
   Compare (1,3) vs (2,2): 1<2, so (1,3) comes before (2,2)
   Compare (3,0) vs (1,1): 3>1, so (1,1) comes first
   Final sorted order: [(1,1), (1,3), (2,2), (3,0), (4,4)]
Step 4: Extract values: [1, 1, 2, 3, 4]
Note: The two 1's maintain their original relative order (index 1 before index 3)

Step 1: Original array: [('b',2), ('a',1), ('b',1), ('a',2)]
Step 2: Create augmented array: [(('b',2),0), (('a',1),1), (('b',1),2), (('a',2),3)]
Step 3: Sort using custom comparator:
   First compare by tuple elements: ('a',1) < ('a',2) < ('b',1) < ('b',2)
   For equal first elements: ('a',1) and ('a',2) both have 'a', compare second elements 1<2
   For ('b',1) and ('b',2) both have 'b', compare second elements 1<2
   Since all tuples are unique, indices don't affect order
   But if we had identical tuples, indices would preserve original order
Step 4: Sorted augmented: [(('a',1),1), (('a',2),3), (('b',1),2), (('b',2),0)]
Step 5: Extract values: [('a',1), ('a',2), ('b',1), ('b',2)]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Custom Comparator with Index Tie-breaking
ladders.desc: Implement the comparator function that enables stable sorting through augmentation
ladders.explanation: The custom comparator is the heart of making an unstable sort stable through augmentation. The intuition is that when two elements have equal primary values, we need a deterministic way to decide their order that preserves their original sequence. By comparing their original indices as a secondary key, we ensure that elements appearing earlier in the original array maintain their position relative to later equal elements. The comparator takes two augmented elements (value, index) and returns: -1 if first < second, 0 if equal, 1 if first > second. It first compares the primary values. If they differ, it returns based on that comparison. If values are equal, it compares the indices, ensuring the element with the smaller (earlier) index comes first. This guarantees stability because when the sorting algorithm encounters equal values, it will consistently place earlier-occurring elements before later ones. Time complexity for each comparison is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) aside from the input parameters. This feeds into the full problem by providing the comparison logic that makes the unstable sort behave stably. Common mistakes include: comparing indices when values aren't equal (wasteful), forgetting that indices should only break ties, implementing the comparator incorrectly for the specific sorting algorithm's API, or not handling edge cases like negative values or custom objects. The key insight is that stability is essentially about preserving a total order, and by making each element unique through (value, index) pairs, we eliminate ambiguity in comparisons.

examples.output: 1 (because 5=5, then compare indices 2>0, so first > second)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Compare primary values: 5 == 5 (equal)
Step 2: Since values equal, compare secondary indices
Step 3: Index 2 > index 0
Step 4: Therefore (5,2) > (5,0)
Step 5: In sorting, (5,0) would come before (5,2), preserving original order

Step 1: Compare primary values: 3 < 7
Step 2: Since 3 < 7, we immediately return -1
Step 3: No need to check indices because values differ
Step 4: In sorting, (3,1) would come before (7,4) based on value alone

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Augmentation with Original Indices
ladders.desc: Transform elements into (value, index) pairs to enable stable sorting
ladders.explanation: Element augmentation is the technique of transforming each array element into a pair containing both the original value and its index. The intuition is that unstable sorting algorithms may reorder equal elements arbitrarily because they treat them as identical. By augmenting each element with its original position, we make every element unique - even elements with the same value become distinguishable by their index. This allows us to use any sorting algorithm while controlling the order of equal elements. The algorithm is: 1) Create an empty array for augmented elements, 2) Iterate through the original array with index i, 3) For each element arr[i], create a pair (arr[i], i), 4) Store this pair in the augmented array. After sorting these augmented pairs, we can extract just the values to get a stably sorted result. The time complexity is O(n) for the augmentation pass. Space complexity is O(n) for the augmented array. This feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the augmented elements that the comparator will work with. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing when the sorting algorithm expects 0-based, modifying the original array in place instead of creating new pairs, not preserving the index properly during the augmentation, or trying to augment after sorting (which defeats the purpose). The key insight is that we're adding metadata (the index) that doesn't affect the primary sorting order but provides tie-breaking information when needed.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start with empty augmented array: []
Step 2: Process index 0, value 10: create (10,0), add to array → [(10,0)]
Step 3: Process index 1, value 20: create (20,1), add to array → [(10,0), (20,1)]
Step 4: Process index 2, value 10: create (10,2), add to array → [(10,0), (20,1), (10,2)]
Step 5: Result: [(10,0), (20,1), (10,2)]
Note: The two 10s are now distinguishable by their indices 0 and 2

Step 1: Empty augmented array: []
Step 2: Index 0, value 'cat': create ('cat',0), add → [('cat',0)]
Step 3: Index 1, value 'dog': create ('dog',1), add → [('cat',0), ('dog',1)]
Step 4: Result: [('cat',0), ('dog',1)]
Note: Even though values are different, indices are still preserved

ladders.title: Building Block — Understanding Unstable Sorting Algorithms
ladders.desc: Learn how unstable sorts like quicksort work and why they need modification for stability
ladders.explanation: Unstable sorting algorithms are those that do not guarantee to preserve the relative order of equal elements. Common examples include quicksort, heapsort, and selection sort. The intuition behind their instability lies in how they swap or move elements during the sorting process. For instance, in quicksort, when partitioning elements around a pivot, equal elements may end up on either side arbitrarily depending on implementation details. In heapsort, the heapify process doesn't consider original positions. This instability occurs because these algorithms treat equal elements as indistinguishable and may swap them based on efficiency considerations rather than preserving order. Understanding this behavior is crucial because it explains why we need augmentation - we must add information (the index) that makes equal elements distinguishable. The algorithm characteristics: they typically have good time complexity (O(n log n) for quicksort and heapsort on average) but sacrifice stability for performance or memory benefits. Time and space analysis varies by algorithm but is generally efficient. This feeds into the augmentation logic by explaining why we can't use these algorithms directly for stable sorting. Common mistakes include: assuming all sorting algorithms are stable, not recognizing when stability matters in a problem, or trying to modify the unstable algorithm's internal logic instead of using augmentation. The key insight is that instability isn't a bug but a design choice - these algorithms prioritize other factors over preserving order of equal elements, which we can work around through augmentation.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Choose pivot (e.g., last element 4)
Step 2: Partition: elements < 4: [3(red), 1, 2, 3(blue)], elements >= 4: [4]
Step 3: Recursively sort left partition [3(red), 1, 2, 3(blue)]
Step 4: Choose pivot 3(blue)
Step 5: Partition: elements < 3: [1, 2], elements >= 3: [3(red), 3(blue)]
Step 6: The two 3s may be swapped during partitioning
Step 7: Final result may have 3(blue) before 3(red) even though red was first originally

Step 1: Build max heap: [5(a), 3, 5(b), 1] becomes heap
Step 2: Extract max (5(a) or 5(b) arbitrarily)
Step 3: Heapify remaining elements
Step 4: Next extract may take the other 5
Step 5: The order of equal elements 5(a) and 5(b) is not preserved
Step 6: Result could be [1, 3, 5(b), 5(a)] even though 5(a) was first

testCases.input: Identify if selection sort is stable

testCases.expected: Partition may swap equal elements arbitrarily
Sorting by grade then by name needs stable sort

testCases.input: Time complexity of quicksort average case

testCases.expected: Does not preserve order of equal elements

testCases.input: Can unstable sorts be made stable?

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pair Creation and Comparison
ladders.desc: Create and compare (value, index) pairs as fundamental operations
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two parts: creating (value, index) pairs and comparing them. The intuition is that we need to bundle data together to carry additional information (the index) along with the original value. Creating a pair is straightforward: we take a value and an index and combine them into a single object. Comparing pairs requires comparing first by value, then by index if values are equal. This is fundamental because it establishes the ordering rules that will make our sorting stable. The algorithm for comparison: given two pairs (a, i) and (b, j), return -1 if a < b, or if a == b and i < j; return 1 if a > b, or if a == b and i > j; return 0 if a == b and i == j. Time complexity for both operations is O(1). Space complexity for creating a pair is O(1) for the pair object. This feeds into the building block by providing the concrete operations needed to implement augmentation. Common mistakes include: comparing indices when values aren't equal (inefficient), not handling different data types properly in comparisons, creating pairs with wrong indices, or misunderstanding how pair comparison defines total order. The key insight is that by creating these pairs and defining their comparison rules, we're essentially creating a new data type with its own ordering that respects both value and original position.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Take value 7
Step 2: Take index 2
Step 3: Combine into a pair (7, 2)
Step 4: This pair now carries both the value and its original position

Step 1: Compare first elements: 5 == 5 (equal)
Step 2: Since first elements equal, compare second elements
Step 3: 0 < 3
Step 4: Therefore (5, 0) < (5, 3)
Step 5: Result: -1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is Stability in Sorting?
ladders.desc: Understand the basic definition of stable vs unstable sorting
ladders.explanation: Stability in sorting refers to whether a sorting algorithm preserves the relative order of elements that have equal keys. The intuition is simple: if you have two equal elements (like two identical numbers or two students with the same grade), a stable sort guarantees that the one that appeared first in the original array will still appear first after sorting. An unstable sort makes no such guarantee - it may swap equal elements arbitrarily. Think of it like sorting a deck of cards by suit: if you have multiple cards of the same suit, a stable sort keeps them in their original order relative to each other, while an unstable sort might shuffle them. This concept is foundational because many real-world sorting problems require stability. For example, when sorting a list of people first by last name, then by first name, you need a stable sort to maintain the first-name order within each last-name group. The algorithm perspective: stability is a property of sorting algorithms, not a parameter. Some algorithms are inherently stable (like merge sort, insertion sort), while others are unstable (like quicksort, heapsort). Time and space complexity don't directly determine stability. This feeds into the basic operation by explaining why we care about preserving order of equal elements. Common mistakes include: confusing stability with overall correctness, thinking all sorts are stable, or not recognizing when stability matters in a problem. The key insight is that stability is about preserving additional information (the original sequence) beyond just the sorted order.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Original array has two 3s: red (first) and blue (second)
Step 2: After sorting by value, we want [1, 2, 3, 3]
Step 3: Stable sort preserves that red 3 comes before blue 3
Step 4: Unstable sort might swap them, putting blue before red
Step 5: Both results are correctly sorted by value, but only stable preserves original relative order

examples.input: Sort students by grade, then by name within same grade
examples.output: Need stable sort to preserve name order after grade sorting
examples.trace:
Step 1: Original: [Alice(B), Bob(A), Carol(B), David(A)]
Step 2: First sort by grade: [Bob(A), David(A), Alice(B), Carol(B)]
Step 3: Now sort by name within each grade
Step 4: With stable sort: Bob before David (original order), Alice before Carol (original order)
Step 5: With unstable sort: might put David before Bob or Carol before Alice

testCases.expected: Sort by date then by time

testCases.input: What happens to equal elements in unstable sort?
Can you tell if sort was stable from output alone?
testCases.expected: Only if you know original order of equals
No, both produce correctly sorted output
